sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=25, col=42).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Selected bottom green #4. Need to check cross position.
- Plan: Check board for cross location.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The cross is at center (12, 42) which is ABOVE the selected green at (25, 42). This is in the top panel area. The cross is at row 12-15, which is above the top panel's grid (rows 17-22). Wait, that doesn't seem right. Let me re-check.
Looking at the ascii:
Row 12: This is above the visible grid area I printed. The cross is at rows 12-15, which is in the area above row 17.
Wait, looking at the image, the cross appears to be in the top panel, at the top row. Let me look at the ascii more carefully.
Row 17: `SSSSSSSSSBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWGGGGWWWWWWWBbSSSSSSSSSS`
The G border is at row 24-27 for the selected green. The cross is at rows 12-15, which is ABOVE the top panel.
Hmm, but looking at the image, the cross appears to be in the TOP panel, in the top row. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the cross (gray X shape) is in the top panel, at the top row, right-aligned. The selected green (with G border) is in the bottom panel, at the first green position.
So the cross is at approximately row 18-21 in the top panel, and the selected green is at row 25 in the bottom panel. The cross is ABOVE the selected green.
When I click the cross, the selected green will move UP to the cross position, passing through... nothing in between? The top panel green is at (19, 42), which is on the same column. So moving from (25, 42) up to the cross position would pass through the top panel green at (19, 42), consuming it!
Let me check the exact cross position. The gray nodes are at:
- (12, 42), (13, 41), (13, 44), (15, 42)
Wait, row 12 is above the top panel. Let me look at the ascii again.
Row 17 is the top of the top panel. The cross nodes are at rows 12, 13, 15 which are ABOVE row 17. This seems odd.
Actually, I think I miscounted. Let me look at the full ascii output. The lines I printed start at row 23. Let me check rows 10-17.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the cross center is at (12, 42). But the top panel starts at row 17. So the cross is 5 rows above the top panel. This doesn't match the image.
Wait, I think the issue is that the image shows the cross in the top panel, but my row counting is off. Let me just look at the image: the cross is in the top panel, at the top row, right-aligned. The selected green is in the bottom panel.
Let me just click the cross and see what happens. The cross center is at (12, 42) according to segmentation, but that seems wrong. Let me look at the ascii to find the actual cross position.
Looking at the ascii output for rows 23-28:
Row 23: `SSSSSSSSSBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWGGGGWWWWWWWBbSSSSSSSSSS`
Row 24: `SSSSSSSSSBWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWGGNNGGWwwwwWBbSSSSSSSSSS`
Row 25: `SSSSSSSSSBWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWGNNNNGWwwwwWBbSSSSSSSSSS`
Row 26: `SSSSSSSSSBWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWGNNNNGWwwwwWBbSSSSSSSSSS`
Row 27: `SSSSSSSSSBWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWWwwwwWGGNNGGWwwwwWBbSSSSSSSSSS`
Row 28: `SSSSSSSSSBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWGGGGWWWWWWWBbSSSSSSSSSS`
The G border is at rows 23-28, cols 40-45 approximately. The selected green is at rows 24-27, cols 41-44.
But the cross nodes are at rows 12, 13, 15 which are ABOVE row 17. This is confusing. Let me check rows 10-17.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Cross at (12,42) - ABOVE the top panel. Selected green at (25,42). Clicking cross should move green up through top panel green at (19,42), consuming it.
Plan: Click the cross to consume the top panel green.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check rows 10-17 to find cross
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    if 10 <= i <= 17:
        print(f"{i:3d}: {line}")

# Click cross at center (12, 42) based on segmentation
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 13, 'col': 42}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
